coredata.dc:description: Delayed healing and fibrosis at the wound site present significant challenges in the wound care industry, often leading to complications such as infections, chronic wounds, and impaired tissue regeneration. Therefore, there is a critical need for advanced wound dressing materials that promote faster healing, prevent bacterial infections, and support effective tissue repair. This study aims to develop a Lysozyme (Lys)-based wound dressing with enhanced wound closure rates by incorporating nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as a functionalized nanofiller to strengthen its antibacterial properties. The wound dressing, formulated with a carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) crosslinked polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) matrix, creates a porous structure that enhances swelling capacity and water vapor transmission rates (WVTR), while cytotoxicity studies confirm its biocompatibility, showing 100 % cell viability in HCT 116 and MCF7 cell lines. The in vivo wound healing performance of the designed nanocomposite hydrogel reflects complete wound closure in 5 h for Drosophila Melanogaster, aided by the shorter life span and faster metabolic processes in Drosophila, and 14 days in Sprague Dawley rat models. These results qualify the material as a promising candidate for wound dressing applications, bridging the gap between material science and medical science for effective wound management.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Delayed healing and fibrosis at the wound site present significant challenges in the wound care industry, often leading to complications such as infections, chronic wounds, and impaired tissue regeneration. Therefore, there is a critical need for advanced wound dressing materials that promote faster healing, prevent bacterial infections, and support effective tissue repair. This study aims to develop a Lysozyme (Lys)-based wound dressing with enhanced wound closure rates by incorporating nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as a functionalized nanofiller to strengthen its antibacterial properties. The wound dressing, formulated with a carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) crosslinked polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) matrix, creates a porous structure that enhances swelling capacity and water vapor transmission rates (WVTR), while cytotoxicity studies confirm its biocompatibility, showing 100 % cell viability in HCT 116 and MCF7 cell lines. The in vivo wound healing performance of the designed nanocomposite hydrogel reflects complete wound closure in 5 h for Drosophila Melanogaster, aided by the shorter life span and faster metabolic processes in Drosophila, and 14 days in Sprague Dawley rat models. These results qualify the material as a promising candidate for wound dressing applications, bridging the gap between material science and medical science for effective wound management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Effective wound management remains a significant challenge in the medical field, driving extensive research focused on discovering materials that can enhance the wound healing process. Delayed wound healing and fibrosis pose major challenges in wound care, resulting in chronic wounds, increased infection risks, and impaired tissue regeneration. Prolonged healing creates a favourable environment for bacterial colonization, leading to complications such as biofilm formation and delayed tissue repair. Advanced wound dressing materials are essential to accelerate healing by providing an optimal microenvironment that supports cell proliferation, reduces inflammation, and minimizes scar formation. Wound healing is a complex process, involving cellular activities, including the participation of blood cells, cytokines, mediators, and growth factors [1]. Among the various stages of wound healing, the inflammatory phase stands out being the most critical [2]. This phase is essential for establishing an immune barrier that prevents microbial proliferation and triggers molecular events that promote cellular infiltration into the wound site [3]. This stage is essential for warding off bacterial infections and speeding up the healing process. Moreover, inflammation supports phagocytosis, which is necessary for eliminating microorganism, scratched tissues, and other foreign particles [4]. Despite the abundance of wound-healing materials on the market, many lack strong antibacterial properties. Ali et al. developed polylactic acid-based multifunctional nanofibrous patches loaded with three drugs, achieving complete wound closure within three weeks [5]. Similarly, Cheng and colleagues employed epidermal growth factor (EGF) to enhance wound healing, leading to closure within 21 days [6]. These studies highlight the role of drug-based interventions and the relatively prolonged healing process. This acts in the background to support epidermal migration, angiogenesis, and maintaining a moist environment that is conducive towards effective healing. In this context, N-GQDs are emerging as highly promising materials due to their outstanding antibacterial properties, which greatly enhance the inflammatory phase and contribute to optimum wound healing outcomes.

N-GQDs are nanoscale graphene fragments enriched with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic functional groups. The incorporation of nitrogen atoms into their structure is a highly effective strategy for enhancing the chemical, electronic, and structural properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [7]. This modification greatly enhances their effectiveness in antibacterial and environmental applications, making them particularly valuable in these areas. Recently, N-GQDs have been employed as nanofillers due to their remarkable attributes, including a large surface area, edge-localized functional groups, solubility in various solvents, chemical inertness, biocompatibility, strong fluorescence activity, and resistance to photobleaching [8–10]. N-GQDs exhibit superior antibacterial properties compared to nitrogen-free GQDs, largely due to nitrogen doping, which enhances their two-photon properties called two-photon photodynamic therapy [11]. Nitrogen functionalization enhances electron donation and expands the π-conjugation system, improving charge transfer between N-GQDs and bacteria, which results in a powerful bactericidal effect. Moreover, N-GQDs induce membrane stress in bacterial cells, causing the disruption of the bacterial cell membrane due to the resemblance between the Gaussian surface of bacterial cell membrane [12]. N-GQDs also inflict oxidative damage on bacterial cells via a superoxide anion-dependent mechanism. By adhering to the cell wall, they release reactive oxygen species (ROS), further contributing to bacterial cell damage [13].

Lysozyme is a protein integral to the immune system, known for its potent bactericidal efficacy. It consists of a single polypeptide chain with 129 amino acids, forming a compact globular structure with Asp52 and Glu35 as its active sites [14]. Additionally, lysozyme offers added benefits in supporting bladder health, managing inflammation, and promoting wound repair [15]. Lysozyme along with N-GQD provide essential microphage to the wound site which enhance the immunity by destroying bacteria, removing foreign materials and increasing immune responses. Lysozyme kills bacteria by hydrolysing peptidoglycan. It cleaves the β-1,4-glycosidic bonds between N-acetylmuramic acid (NAM) and N-acetylglucosamine (NAG) in the bacterial cell's peptidoglycan layer [16]. During the inflammatory process, the wound secretes acidic compounds that can slow down healing and lead to unhealthy inflammation [17]. Lysozyme helps neutralize these acidic compounds, creating a neutral environment at the wound site, thereby accelerating the healing process. Lysozyme also enhances cytokine and adenosine production [18]. Meanwhile, carboxymethyl cellulose excels in clotting due to its haemostatic properties and its ability to absorb large volumes of liquid [19]. This process results in the formation of a clot over the wound surface, where blood collagen interacts with proteins to form a network, leading to efficient blood clotting [20]. Maintaining a dry environment and absorbing wound fluid are crucial aspects of the healing process. The combined hydrogel of CMC and PVP exhibits exceptional swelling behaviour and a high vapor transmission rate, which aids in keeping the wound surface dry [21]. PVP, renowned for its excellent film-forming properties, enhances the tensile strength, flexibility, and adhesive qualities of the nanocomposite when blended with CMC [22].

In this work, we have developed innovative nanocomposite hydrogels by incorporating nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), lysozyme protein, carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) through a green synthesis approach. This method reflects our commitment to sustainable and eco-friendly practices, introducing a novel solution for wound healing while addressing the limitations of conventional treatments. Green synthesis emphasizes the use of environmentally friendly, sustainable processes and materials [23]. Consistent with this principle, our synthesis utilized non-toxic, biodegradable agents and water as a safe, eco-friendly solvent. The resulting nanocomposite hydrogel is biodegradable and free of harmful residues, aligning with the objectives of green chemistry and offering a sustainable, biocompatible product for effective wound healing applications. These hydrogels are designed to offer enhanced nutritional benefits and exhibit significantly improved antibacterial properties, positioning them as a highly promising solution for more effective wound care. The PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogel works on the primary hypothesis of prevention of fibrosis resulted from excessive collagen deposition, faster production of epithelial cells as well as unprecedented bactericidal behaviour, exerted by Lysozyme and N-GQD, for achieving accelerated wound healing. The as-synthesized nanocomposites not only demonstrate exceptional biocompatibility and non-toxic characteristics but also exhibit robust antibacterial activity. Moreover, they have shown remarkable wound healing potential across both vertebrate (rats) and invertebrate models (drosophila), highlighting their applicability and effectiveness towards wound healing in higher organisms.

PVP was acquired from Sigma Aldrich Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. in Bangalore, India, exhibits an average molecular weight (Mw) of 40,000 and a purity of 99.5 %. The CMC and Lysozyme, boasting a purity of 98 %, was sourced from CDH Chemicals, New Delhi, India. Glutaraldehyde (GA) (25 % solution), possessing a molecular weight of 100.12, served as the crosslinker in the hydrogel formation process is obtained from Sigma Aldrich Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. in Bangalore, India. Dextrose, featuring a molecular weight of 180.16 g/mol, was bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Concentrated ammonia solution (28–30 %) with a molecular mass of 35.05 g/mol was acquired from CDH Chemicals, India. Sodium hydroxide, Hydrochloric acid and pH buffer tablets are obtained from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, Maharashtra, India. The chemicals used from wound fluid preparations such as NaCl, KCl, NaHCO3 and CaCl2 are bought from MERCK chemical company, Mumbai, India. MMT and LB agar, used in cytotoxicity study and zone inhibition antibacterial application, is obtained from Sigma Aldrich Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. in Bangalore, India.

The nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) is prepared by a series of precise steps (Scheme S1) [24]. As a carbon precursor 5 g of dextrose is taken and dissolved in 50 ml of Millipore water. During stirring 2.5 ml of ammonia solution is mixed in the solution. This mixture is then subjected to heating at different temperatures (100, 150, 200, 250 and 300° Celsius) for half an hour in a muffle furnace, resulting in the development of a transparent pale-yellow solution. Subsequently, the solution is vigorously stirred to remove the residual ammonia. After that, pH 7 is maintained by adding NaOH/HCl solution dropwise using a basic pH meter. Following this, the N-GQD solution is passed through a microporous membrane filter, typically with a pore size of around 0.22 μm. The membrane filter ensures that larger impurities or aggregated particles are retained, while the smaller particles pass through resulting in preparation of N-GQDs.

A series of PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels are prepared with different quantities of PVP and CMC along with different amount of crosslinker through a straightforward process involving solution mixing of PVP and CMC, followed by the addition of Lysozyme and N-GQD as detailed in Scheme S2. Initially, a conical flask containing 10 ml of double distilled water is taken, into which 0.1 g, 0.2 g, 0.3 g, 0.4 g and 0.5 g of pure PVP is introduced and stirred at 70 °C for 4 h to make 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%, 4 wt% and 5 wt% PVP solution respectively. Subsequently, previously prepared CMC solutions of 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%, 4 wt% and 5 wt% are added to the mixture, stirred and sonicated at a frequency 40 KHz for 15 min to ensure a uniform dispersion, the variation of concentration is listed in Table S1. Then 3 ml of 1 wt% Lysozyme solution was added to the solution with constant stirring. Next, the previously prepared N-GQD solution is carefully added drop by drop into the well-mixed and homogeneous combination of PVP, CMC and Lysozyme, with continuous stirring. After 2 h of stirring, the crosslinker GA is introduced into the mixture and left to stir for an additional 24 h, maintaining a constant temperature of 45 to 50 °C. The resulting homogeneously dispersed viscous nanocomposite suspension is then poured into glass petri dishes and left to incubate at 40 °C overnight to get dried PVP/CMC-LYS@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films.

Various advanced characterization techniques are utilized to examine the microstructure of N-GQD nanoparticles, PVP/CMC and PVP/CMC-Lys composite, and PVP/CMC-Lys @N-GQD nanocomposite. Particle size and distribution are determined through dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements conducted using a Zettaliter Nano Instrument (Malvern), model number ZEN-3690. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) with FEI Tecnai T20 200 keV and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with Gemini-SEM 450 (Zeiss) are employed to visualize surface morphology and distribution of nano-phases like N-GQD over the composite surface. The crystal structure, crystallinity size and orientation of the hydrogel are studied by XRD with the Rigaku D z Max-Ra rotating anode instrument. The chemical composition and molecular structure of the synthesized materials are analysed using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, conducted with a Perkin-Elmer FTIR spectrometer operating within a frequency range of 4000 to 500 cm−1. Optical properties of N-GQDs are investigated through UV–vis spectroscopy using a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–Visible spectrophotometer, measuring wavelengths between 200 and 600 nm. Additionally, fluorescence spectroscopy is performed using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies) to gain insights into the optical behaviour of N-GQDs, in which emission spectra of N-GQD is observed in different excitation wavelengths.

The gel content or gel fraction indicates the degree of crosslinking within a hydrogel's polymer network. To measure the gel fraction, the hydrogel is first brought to its optimal swelling state and then dehydrated. The calculation of the gel fraction involves using a particular formula [25].
(1)
GF
%
=
Mf / Mi
× 100 %
In this context, GF denotes the gel fraction, Mf represents the weight of the dried sample, and Mi signifies the weight of the initial hydrogel prior to drying.

The effectiveness of hydrogels in wound healing is significantly influenced by their swelling properties. To assess the swelling capacity, the weight change is recorded from the initial dried hydrogel samples to their fully swollen state at various time points. Uniformly shaped dried hydrogel samples, each weighing between 0.1 g to 0.2 g, are submerged in 100 ml of double-distilled water maintained at room temperature (25 °C). At predetermined intervals, the samples are extracted, gently blotted with filter paper to remove surface water, and weighed. This procedure is repeated until the hydrogel samples' weights stabilized, indicating they had reached their maximum swelling capacity [26]. The degree of swelling, or swelling ratio, is then calculated using a specific formula.
(2)
Swelling
%
=
Wt − Wi
/ Wi × 100 %

The initial weight Wi and the weight at time Wt are used to calculate the swelling ratio. To assess the pH-responsive swelling behaviour of the nanocomposites, their swelling capacities are measured in buffer solutions at pH levels 4, 7 and 10. The pH of these buffer solutions is checked every hour with a pH meter and adjusted as necessary throughout the experiments. This approach provides valuable data on the nanocomposites responses to varying pH environments. Additionally, understanding the swelling behaviour in wound fluids is crucial for effective moisture management on wound surfaces. For this purpose, a simulated wound fluid is prepared by dissolving 5.844 g of NaCl, 0.298 g of KCl, 3.360 g of NaHCO3, and 0.227 g of CaCl2 in 1000 ml of deionized water. Both composite and nanocomposite hydrogels are then immersed in this solution to evaluate their swelling characteristics under standard temperature and pressure conditions [27].

Maintaining appropriate moisture levels is crucial for both open and wet wounds, making the water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) a key parameter in evaluating wound healing materials. To measure the WVTR of composites and nanocomposites, the standard water method is followed. Samples of each hydrogel are precisely cut and affixed to the openings of identical cylindrical glass vials with a radius of 1.4 cm. Each vial is filled with 25 ml of distilled water and sealed securely with paraffin tape. These vials are then incubated at 40 °C, and the water weight is recorded after 24 h. This measurement process is repeated three times over 5 days to enhance accuracy. The WVTR of the hydrogel is calculated using a specific formula (Eq. (3)), providing essential insights into the material's capacity to control moisture, which is critical for wound healing applications [28].
(3)
WVTR =
− ∆ W
Change in the amount of the water weight
A
Area of exposure
× ∆ T
Time
mgcm − 2 h − 1

The cytotoxicity of PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD is studied with HCT 116 which is Colon Carcinoma cell and MCF 7 (Breast Cancer cell) using MTT assay [29]. For this MTT (3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) stock solution of 1 mg/ml is prepared. For MTT assay both HCT 116 and MCF 7 cells are seeded in 96 well culture plates with a density of 1000 cells per well in 180 μL of growth medium and incubated for 24 h at 40 °C. Each culture medium various concentration of nanocomposites (0.5 v/v, 1 v/v, 5 v/v, 10 v/v, 25 v/v and 50 v/v) and further incubated for 24 h. After the incubation period, the wells are treated with 0.1 ml of MMT stock solution and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C, after this the medium is removed and washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS). For the cell viability study, it is dissolved in 1.5 ml dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and the absorbances are recorded at 540 nm, and percentage of cell viability is measured by using the equation.
(4)
Cell Viability
%
=
Absorbance
a t
450 nm
Sample
Absorbance
at
450 nm
Control
× 100

The antibacterial effectiveness of the synthesized PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD is evaluated against E. coli, a Gram-negative bacterium, using the agar well diffusion assay. To conduct this analysis, LB agar plates are prepared, and a specific volume of bacterial culture is evenly spread across the surface. The composites and nanocomposites are introduced into four quadrants of the agar plates at concentrations of 40 μL, 60 μL, 80 μL, and 100 μL. The plates are then incubated at a constant temperature of 37 °C for 16 h. Following incubation, the inhibition zones are measured to assess the antibacterial activity.

The inhibition of bacterial biofilm formation is analysed using Propidium Iodide (PI) and DAPI staining. To facilitate biofilm growth, LB broth is added to a well plate along with a coverslip. Then 60 μL and 100 μL of samples are introduced followed by the addition of E. coli bacterial strains. The plates are incubated at 37 °C for 48 h to allow biofilm formation. After incubation, PI and DAPI staining are performed to visualize the inhibition zones on the coverslips. The coverslips are carefully removed from the well plate, washed with PBS, and subsequently stained with PI and DAPI. Finally, the stained samples are examined under a confocal microscope to evaluate the antibacterial activity of the tested samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing assay (Drosophila melanogaster)
ce:section.ce:para:
The wound healing process is an intrinsic and intricate phenomenon that encompasses various stages and involves diverse cell types, both in humans and Drosophila. Effective tissue clotting and epithelization following an injury are essential for promoting rapid recovery. Given the remarkable similarity in gene conservation, with around 65 % to 70 % of genes being conserved between Drosophila and humans [30], it is unsurprising that the genes implicated in wound healing are shared in both species. Consequently, Drosophila serves as an excellent model for studying the underlying mechanisms of wound healing. For the wound healing study, wounds are induced using insulin needles, and the generated samples are then applied to the wounds. The wound healing process is evaluated by comparing control flies with flies treated with the sample. Observations are made using a stereo microscope, and photographs are taken at various time intervals up to 6 h. Subsequently, the images are processed using ImageJ software to assess the closure progress of the wounds during each time interval (ranging from 1 to 6 h). The extent of wound closure is measured to compare the healing response between the control flies and the flies treated with the applied samples. In these time intervals, wound closure percentage is measured using the equation.
(5)
Wound Clouser
%
=
Wound area in inntial time − Wound area after time
t
Wound area in inntial time
× 100
%

ce:section.ce:section-title: Body weight analysis of adult flies
ce:section.ce:para: 3–4 days old adult flies are taken in 4 different groups (n = 20 in each group). Each group of flies are anesthetized and a wound is induced in each fly at their thorax region while being anesthetized. In the first group of flies the body weight of the flies is measured before wound induction and is considered as control body weight. After that wound is induced and the flies are again transferred to their standard food medium for 5 h without any treatment. In rest 3 groups of flies after the wound is induced the respective compounds PVP/CMC, PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD are applied topically on their wound surface as a treatment. Then flies from all the groups were transferred to different controlled food mediums for 5 h. After 5 h flies were again collected from all the experimental groups, anesthetized and their body weight was measured in group (n = 20) to check if there are any physiological changes after the wound.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing assay (Sprague-Dawley rats)
ce:section.ce:para: For the in vivo wound healing analysis in vertebrates, 15 male white Sprague-Dawley rats weighing between 300 g to 350 g are utilized. These rats are divided into three groups: the Control group and groups treated with PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels, and they are allowed to acclimate for a week. All experimental procedures are conducted following the approved protocol by the Animals Ethics Committee of Veer Surendra Sai Institute of Medical Science and Research (VSSIMSAR). Throughout the study, the rats are individually housed under controlled conditions, maintaining a temperature of 37 °C and humidity at 40 %. They are exposed to a 12-h alternate dark-light cycle. Prior to the experiment, the rats are anesthetized with 70 mg/kg of ketamine, administered intraperitoneally. To create the wound, the dorsal region of the rats was carefully shaved using Veet® hair removal cream, and the exposed skin was sterilized with ethanol. A full-thickness wound, 8 mm to 10 mm in length, was created in this area. For the Treatment group, the wounds are covered with a thin film of nanocomposite, which was securely fixed using micropore tape. A sterile gauze was applied to cover the entire treated area post-treatment. In contrast, the wounds in the Control group received no additional treatment. To assess the wound area closure, a systematic approach is employed. At specific intervals of 0, 3, 7, 10, and 14 days after wound infliction, the wound surface is carefully traced using a transparent graph sheet. During this process, the nanocomposite films are removed to enable accurate measurement. Subsequently, the wounds are cleaned and photographed to facilitate comparison. To quantify the wound closure, the area of the wounds is precisely calculated utilizing advanced ImageJ software. Employing Eq. (5), we were able to derive accurate measurements and track the progress of wound healing over the designated time points. This systematic and standardized approach allowed us to comprehensively assess the efficacy of the nanocomposite films in promoting wound closure and provided valuable insights for our investigation.

To investigate the photophysical properties of N-GQDs, UV–visible spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectra are employed as key analytical tools. The absorption spectra of N-GQDs, depicted in Fig. 1(a) display two distinct absorption peaks at 321 nm and 338 nm [31]. The absorption peak at 321 nm is associated with π-π* electronic transitions, which are characteristic of the aromatic CC bonds present in the graphene-like structure of the N-GQDs. This transition indicates the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms within the aromatic domains of the quantum dots. On the other hand, the absorption peak at 338 nm is attributed to n-π* electronic transitions. These transitions are indicative of the involvement of lone pair electrons from heteroatoms, such as oxygen and nitrogen, interacting with the conjugated π-system. Specifically, the n-π* transition involves functional groups like carbonyl (C=O) and imine or pyridinic nitrogen (C=N), which are introduced during the doping process [32,33]. This detailed spectral information provides critical insights into the electronic structure and chemical composition of the N-GQDs. Fig. 1(b) presents an intriguing deviation from typical emission spectra, showing distinct peaks with varying intensities under different excitation wavelengths. The shift of fluorescence spectra towards longer wavelengths is referred to as the “red-edge effect.” This phenomenon arises from alterations in atomic arrangements, particularly those influenced by interactions between the solute and the solvent [34]. These atomic rearrangements, driven by solute-solvent interactions, are commonly described as solvent dynamics [35]. The variation in fluorescence intensities observed at different wavelengths indicate that N-GQDs can be engineered to emit a broad spectrum of colours. By adjusting the excitation wavelengths, it is possible to fine-tune the emission properties, thereby enabling precise control over the colour output of N-GQDs. This tunability makes them highly versatile to be applicable in various fields, from bioimaging and sensing to optoelectronic devices, where specific emission characteristics are crucial [36].

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of PVP, CMC, Lysozyme, and prepared N-GQD are presented in the Fig. 1(c). The N-GQD spectrum shows an intense OH stretching band at 3408 cm−1, attributed to the presence of the hydrogen bond formed due to the presence of OH and NH groups [37]. The characteristic peaks of N-GQD include a peak at 1398 cm−1, attributed to the symmetric stretching vibration of C–NH of aromatic amine, indicating successful nitrogen atom doping. Additionally, a sharp band at 1654 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching modes of conjugated aromatic hydrocarbons and 1052 cm−1 corresponds to CO of primary alcohol groups [38]. The FTIR spectra of PVP reveal a characteristic peak at 1272 cm−1, corresponding to CN stretching vibrations, which are indicative of the pyrrolidone ring present in the PVP structure. In addition to this intense peak at 1652 cm−1 is due to presence of CO stretching vibration of pyrrolidone ring and other peaks at 2973 cm−1, and 1411 cm−1 are attributed to CH stretching, and CH2 bending vibrations, respectively [39]. In the CMC spectra there are characteristic peaks at 1640 cm−1 for carbonyl group present in the carboxymethyl substituents of the CMC structure, 2923 cm−1 for CH stretch of methylene and 1030 cm−1 for C–O–C group of 1,4-β-D-glucoside linkage in the cellulose backbone [40]. While the lysozyme shows peaks at 1690 cm−1 due to CO of α-helical structures present in the long amino acid chain.

The FTIR spectra of the PVP/CMC gel are illustrated in the Fig. 1(d), showing changes in the characteristic peaks of the individual components, providing evidence of molecular interactions between PVP and CMC. The distinct peak observed at 1652 cm−1 in PVP, attributed to the CO stretching vibration of its pyrrolidone rings, and the peak at 1640 cm−1 in CMC merge into a single broad peak in the gel spectrum resulting in formation of an interpenetrating network structure [41]. When Lysozyme protein is incorporated into the PVP/CMC matrix, a new peak at 1702 cm−1, corresponding to the CO stretching of Lysozyme, appears along with the broad peak of the PVP/CMC gel. Upon the addition of N-GQDs, a strong -OH band appears at 3290 cm−1, attributed to hydrogen bonding interactions, confirming the grafting of N-GQDs onto the PVP/CMC-Lys composite [42]. The spectra retain all other characteristic peaks, signifying the structural integrity of the components within the nanocomposite. These observations collectively validate the successful fabrication of the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite, with enhanced interactions between the components. The probable mechanism for the interaction is shown in Scheme 1 .

The X-ray diffractograms depicted in Fig. S1 illustrates the crystallinity properties of the synthesized PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite films. Fig. S1(a) clearly establishes the semicrystalline peak of PVP/CMC blend appearing at 2θ value of 20.54°. Similar observations have been reported by Morsi et al. [43]. The other relatively smaller halo peak, attributing for PVP, at around 10° is supressed due to the enzymatic action of the incorporated Lysozyme. This suppression is attributed to the enzymatic action of the incorporated Lysozyme, which interacts with the polymer matrix and alters its structural arrangement. The sharp crystalline peaks displayed at 2θ values of 38.4°, 44.6°, 78.1° and 82.38° may appear for certain crystalline sects in the Lysozyme structure highlighting the protein's inherent ordered domains contributing to the overall composite crystallinity. In Fig. S1(b), the XRD pattern of PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite films suggest the change in crystallinity of the PVP/CMC peak appearing at a higher 2θ value of 21.45°, due to the incorporation of N-GQD into the PVP/CMC-Lys matrix. A crystalline peak at 2θ = 36.5° indicates the diffraction peak of the lattice structures of N-GQD. This peak represents the (002) plane of GQD which is in agreement with JCPDS Card No: 75–0444. Similar diffraction peak is reported in literature [44]. The respective d-spacing (d) and crystallite sizes (τ) of individual peaks obtained for PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite films, calculated using Braggs equation and Debye Scherrer equation, are represented in tabular form in Fig. S1(a and b). A clear decrease in crystallite size can be marked upon incorporation of Lysozyme and N-GQD which corresponds to the rise in crystallinity. This may be due to proper exfoliation of nanofillers along the PVP/CMC-Lysozyme networks. Similar lowering of crystallite sizes upon loading of N-GQD has been reported by Nejad et al. [45].

A more detailed perspective regarding elemental composition and chemical interactions between the components of the as-synthesized PVP/CMC-LYS@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogel is provided by XPS analysis (Fig. 2 ). The XPS scan in Fig. 2(a) represents the major peaks obtained corresponding to binding energy of C 1 s, O 1 s and N 1 s at 284.0, 531.0 and 399.0 eV, respectively. Further the high-resolution core spectra achieved for individual elements are deconvoluted in Fig. 2 (b-d) into distinct peaks, representing various interactions. In Fig. 2(b), the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum is deconvoluted into three well-defined peaks located at binding energies of 283.9 eV, 285.0 eV, and 287.8 eV. These peak at 283.9 eV assigned to the aliphatic C atoms (C—C/C—H) which correspond hydrogen backbone, 285.0 eV attribute to CO of CMC and 287.8 eV represents NH—CHR—CO moieties of amino acids and the peptidic CO bonds, respectively [46,47]. The broad peak for O 1 s (Fig. 2(c)) is deconvoluted into three distinct components, using Gaussian fit, at 530.7, 531.0 and 532.0 eV which stands for O=C—N, C—O—C and C—O—H binding energies, respectively. The peak at 530.7 eV represents O=C—N bonds, showing amide and carbonyl contribution, peak at 531.0 eV corresponds to C—O—C ether linkages present in CMC structure. The peak at 532.0 eV is due to C—O—H bonds, which is due to hydroxyl groups of protein structures [48]. This establishes the crosslinking of CMC strands using glutaraldehyde. Fig. 2(d) shows the deconvolution of N 1 s peak yielding two peaks at binding energy values of 399.2 and 401.0 eV. These peaks can be assigned to the amine and amide groups present in LYS, while the peak at 401.0 eV typically stands for nitrogen present in organic matrices (NH and CN bonds). This evidences the protein loading onto the hybrid polymeric strands [46].

To evaluate surface morphology and homogeneity SEM image of PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite is depicted in Fig. S2. The SEM image in Fig. S2(a and b) expose a rough surface with non-uniform porosity, indicating the structural complexity of the nanocomposite [49,50]. Taking the closer analysis, the enlarged images in Fig. S2(c and d) indicate increased porosity and visible platelets like structure all over the material surface. The presence of black spots in these images is attributed to varying shades within the polymeric matrix layers. The distinct morphology of the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite is especially clear in Fig. S2(e and f), where the interplay of the polymeric components with the incorporated N-GQDs is clearly observed. These findings suggest that the dispersion of N-GQD successfully enhances surface uniformity and improves interactions within the nanocomposite hydrogel. Furthermore, elemental mapping conducted for carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen demonstrates the homogeneous distribution of these elements throughout the material, with no visible signs of agglomeration [51]. This uniform elemental dispersion serves as strong evidence of the well-maintained pore structure, ensuring the functional stability and structural integrity of the nanocomposite.

The Fig. 3 depicts the microscopic investigation of PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite and its magnified image in Fig. 3(b, c and d) using HRTEM microscopy. The uniform dispersion of non-uniform shaped and sized N-GQD within the nanocomposite is well established in Fig. 3(a) [52]. The figure gives an overview of distribution of N-GQD without any agglomeration which plays a critical role in the structural and functional property of material. Fig. 3(e) illustrates the magnified micrographs where the individual dimensions of N-GQD can be studied by showing a single N-GQD. The lattice fringe widths are analysed in Fig. 3(f and g) and are found to be 0.24 nm and 0.22 nm with two selected regions along with its FFT patterns, providing evidence of the crystalline nature of the N-GQDs [53]. In Fig. 3(h), the selected area diffraction (SAED) image of the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite shows the crystallinity of the as-synthesized nanocomposite hydrogel induced by N-GQDs. The diffraction rings and spots observed in the SAED image signify the ordered arrangement of atoms in the composite, with the crystallinity being notably influenced by the incorporation of N-GQDs [54]. The particle size distribution, determined using ImageJ software from Fig. 3(a),is represented in the form of a histogram in Fig. 3(i). The histogram indicates that the average particle size is approximately 27 nm, offering critical data on the nanoscopic dimensions of the N-GQDs within the composite [55]. The particle size obtained from the microscopic analysis is similar to the result of dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis. The DLS analysis tells the hydrodynamic radius of the nanoparticle is found to be 25 mm to 29 nm, measured with five consecutive measurements [56]. Importantly, the solvent and synthesis temperature play a significant role in influencing the particle size of the N-GQDs, as highlighted in Fig. S3. These parameters are crucial during the synthesis process as they directly affect the growth kinetics and aggregation tendencies of the nanoparticles. The electron microscopy analyses offer valuable insights into the dispersion, size, and crystallinity of the N-GQDs within the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite.

The gel fraction and swelling behaviour are critical factors in determining the applicability of hydrogels. The swelling behaviour of nanocomposite hydrogel is compared with different ratios of matrix, crosslinker, pH level and wound fluid. In Fig. S4(a), swelling behaviour of PVP/CMC, PVP/CMC-Lys composite and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels are compared. The nanocomposite demonstrates that the inclusion of nanoparticles increases swelling by approximately 510 % within 8 h [57]. The N-GQD helps the nanocomposite to trap more water in its matrix facilitated by the functional groups present in the N-GQD particles. As depicted in the Fig. S4(b), the swelling behaviour is also influenced by the ratio of PVP to CMC, with the nanocomposite exhibiting maximum swelling with a PVP/CMC ratio of 2:1. Different amounts of crosslinker also influenced the swelling capacity of the nanocomposite. PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD hydrogels with 2 ml of crosslinking agent show maximum swelling and with increase in amount of crosslinking the swelling capacity decreases (Fig. S4(c)). This reduction in swelling percentage is due to development of tight networks formed between the polymeric network which restricts the entry of water trapped between them.

The pH-dependent swelling of the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite was studied under three different pH conditions (Fig. S4(d)). The swelling behaviour is influenced by the ionization of functional groups, which in turn depends on the nature of the solvent. The swelling behaviour of the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite is influenced by the pH of the solvent, which influences the association and dissociation of hydrogen bonds formed in PVP-CMC and PVP-CMC-Lys. Lysozyme, being a protein, has long chains of amino acids that include both amino and acidic groups. Under acidic conditions, the higher concentration of hydrogen ions leads to reduced ionization of the carboxylic groups, resulting in less space for water absorption. Consequently, the hydrogel becomes less hydrophilic, reducing its swelling capacity. Conversely, as the pH increases, more functional groups become available to hold water molecules, thereby increasing the swelling ratio. The nanocomposite hydrogel exhibits maximum swelling at pH 10, reaching up to 590 % [58]. The Fig. S4(e) compares swelling behaviour in simulated wound fluid swelling and distilled water, highlighting the importance of absorbing wound exudate for rapid healing. Remarkably, the nanocomposite swells up to approximately 650 % in wound fluid. To assess the water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) or water uptake by the nanocomposite, a WVTR test was conducted over three consecutive days. The typical WVTR for wounded skin ranges from 1.16 to 21.41 mgcm−2 h−1 [59]. The PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite maintains a WVTR within the range of 8–10 mgcm−2 h−1 over the three days (Fig. S4(f)), making it suitable for effectively regulating fluids at the wound site. The gel fraction provides insight into the degree of crosslinking within the hydrogel structure. To measure the gel fraction of the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel, varying weight percentages of PVP and CMC, along with different amounts of crosslinker were examined in Fig. S4(g-i). The gel fraction of nanocomposite hydrogels typically decreases as the concentration of PVP and CMC increases. Notably, the gel content experiences a more pronounced decline with higher concentrations of CMC. Additionally, increasing the amount of crosslinking agent further reduces the gel content of the nanocomposite hydrogel.

The as-synthesized nanocomposites are evaluated for their toxicological implications on living cells before extending them as in vivo wound dressings. The Fig. 4 (a, b and c) represent the in vitro and in vivo cytotoxicity assays conducted on HCT116, MCF7 cell lines, DAPI fluorescent staining and Trypan blue staining in drosophila larvae. MTT assay on both HCT116 and MCF7 cells (Fig. 4(a)) show that the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels maintain a significantly high level of cell viability, with the minimum cell viability observed being approximately 80 %, even at higher concentrations. This level of cell viability meets the criteria outlined in ISO 10993-5 (GB/T 16886.5–2003) standards [60], which set the acceptable limits for in vitro cytotoxicity testing of biomaterials. According to these standards, cell viability above 70 % is considered non-cytotoxic, confirming that the nanocomposite hydrogel is safe for biological applications. However, from the cell viability study, it can be clearly concluded that PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogel shows 100 % cell viability at 5 % v/v concentration. This indicates that at this concentration, the nanocomposite not only avoids cytotoxic effects but also provides an environment conducive to cell survival and possibly proliferation. This enhancement of the population of viable cells at higher concentration suggests the high biocompatibility and qualifies the material for in vivo trials [61]. The in vivo cytotoxicity effect of the as-synthesized materials on drosophila larvae is studied using DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) and trypan blue staining (Fig. 4(b and c)). DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) is a fluorescent stain that binds to the DNA of nuclei, allowing visualization of nuclear morphology [62]. The DAPI staining in Fig. 4(b) reflects almost negligible fragmented nuclei for the treatment groups (PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD) as compared to control. These findings strongly indicate that the hydrogels are non-toxic at the tested concentrations, as they do not cause nuclear fragmentation or induce cellular stress in the larvae. Trypan blue, a vital dye commonly used to detect dead or damaged cells by penetrating compromised membranes, showed no blue-stained cells in the treatment groups (PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD hydrogels) [63]. This lack of staining confirms the absence of significant cell death or toxicity, further highlighting the excellent biocompatibility of the as-synthesized hydrogels. These properties make them highly promising candidates for various biomedical applications [64].

Fig. 4(d and e) depicts the bactericidal activity shown by PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels against E. coli bacterial strain (gram negative) evaluated via zone inhibition method and biofilm inhibition methods. In Fig. 4(d), the inhibition zones obtained after 16 h incubation for PVP/CMC-Lys were found to be very less as compared to PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite. The PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogel exhibits significantly larger inhibition zones, confirming the enhanced bactericidal properties imparted by the incorporation of N-GQDs [65]. This effect further becomes prominent with increase in concentration of the nanocomposite from 40 to 100 μL. The increase in the concentration inhibits higher bacterial growth which highlights the effectiveness of the nanocomposite. The –OH and –NH functionalities present on the N-GQD surface interferes with the anionic bacterial surface and exhibits inhibitory effects [66,67]. These functional groups interact with the negatively charged bacterial surface, disrupting the cell membrane and compromising its structural integrity and functionality resulting in destruction of bacteria. This is also confirmed by the antibiofilm forming activity of PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels (Fig. 4(e)). The PI (Propidium iodide) and DAPI staining results indicate fewer bacterial strains in both PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels as compared to control study where E. coli population is higher. This confirms the material will prevent the formation of bacterial biofilms on the wound surface which is responsible for delayed healing [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing study (Drosophila model)
ce:section.ce:para: Wound healing properties of PVP/CMC, PVP/CMC-Lys composite, and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels were investigated using a Drosophila Melanogaster model. Real-time observations were made to monitor changes in cell arrangements in the wounded drosophila, with particular attention to the formation of actin cables and filopodia projections around the wound surface which are key indicators of wound healing [69]. In Fig. 5 , the presence of epithelial cells surrounding the wound area indicates successful wound contraction. During the initial stage of wound recovery, haemolymph secretion was observed on the wound surface which is a natural stimulus to injury in Drosophila. The secretion of haemolymph was managed by clotting formation within an hour in groups treated with both PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD hydrogels [70]. This rapid clot formation not only prevents excessive fluid loss but also creates a stable environment conducive to tissue repair. After two hours, the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogel demonstrated significant wound closure, achieving approximately 52 % closure (Fig. S5(a)), a result attributed to lysozyme's ability to regulate actin cable formation. This formation of actin cable accelerates the organisation of cells at the wound surface, promoting faster healing [71].

By the third hour, the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD treatment group achieved 68 % wound closure, showing superior healing efficiency compared to 51 % closure observed with the PVP/CMC-Lys composite. The N-GQD in the nanocomposite played a crucial role in enhancing wound healing by inhibiting bacterial growth near the wound surface. In contrast, the control group and the PVP/CMC-treated drosophila showed significantly lower wound healing rates of 24 % and 31 %, respectively [72]. Remarkably, the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD achieved complete wound healing within just 5 h, demonstrating the exceptional wound healing efficacy of the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite. The wound healing measurement shows significant result with high accuracy with P value 0.001, 0.003, 0.001 and 0.001 and standard deviation of 1.127, 4.07, 1.371 and 3.288 for control, PVP/CMC, PVP/CMC-Lys, and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD with an R2 value exceeding 0.99 (Fig. S8). The body weight of flies after 5 h of wound induction was compared with the body weight of flies before wound. However, there was no significant alteration in body weight of flies before and after the wound. In case of flies that have received topical formulation of PVP/CMC, PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD also had no significant changes in body weight (Fig. S6). The improved performance of the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite can be linked to its ability to create an optimal microenvironment around the wound, which supports rapid tissue regeneration, maintains moisture, and prevents bacterial contamination.

Fig. 6 depicts the wound contraction in the control mouse and those treated with the PVP/CMC-Lys composite and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels for a 14-day period following wound infliction. During the first three days, the control group exhibited minimal wound contraction of only 3 %, whereas the groups treated with PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD showed significant wound closure of 21 % and 17 %, respectively (Fig. S5(b)). This demonstrates the enhanced efficiency of PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD in facilitating rapid wound recovery during the early stages. By the fifth day post-injury, the nanocomposite-treated groups showed marked progress in wound healing, with substantial wound contraction and noticeable epithelial tissue development. By the end of the first week, the treated groups achieved 52 % wound closure, significantly outperforming the control group. 10 days after the injury, a clear distinction in wound healing was observed between the treated and control groups. The accelerated healing process in the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD group can be attributed to its ability to eliminate pathogens, maintain a conducive environment around the wound, and support the wound's protein and energy requirements necessary for tissue regeneration and repair. [73]. The moisture retention capacity of PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite provide the ideal environment for chronic and burn wounds. Remarkably, groups treated with PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite show complete re-epithelialization by 14th day. The measurements yielded highly accurate results, with significant P-values of 0.004, 0.003, and 0.001 for the control, PVP/CMC-Lys, and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD, respectively. The corresponding standard deviations were 3.509, 2.772, and 1.888, further supporting the reliability of the data (Fig. S9). These findings demonstrate exceptionally high statistical significance, with an R2 value exceeding 0.99 and a p-value of <0.005. These results indicate a strong correlation and high reliability of our material in promoting wound healing. Given these promising outcomes, future research could focus on scaling up the study to explore its efficacy in potential clinical applications. The findings of in vivo wound healing assay are affirmed by the histopathological studies depicted in Fig. 6(b). The H&E staining study shows the presence of epidermal and dermal appendages on day 0 for all three groups. The formation of granulation tissues along with neovascularization in the granular tissues is reflected on day 7 for both PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels which accounts for accentuated wound closure rates as compared to wounds of control group.

The correlation between Lysozyme release and the formation of granulation tissue and collagen fibrils is analysed in Fig. S7. Fig. S7(a) illustrates the calibration curve for Lysozyme, with the regression equation determined as y = 1.2382× + 0.2966 and a regression coefficient (R2) of 0.9875. These values were used to calculate the release of Lysozyme. Fig. S7(b) presents the absorbance peaks corresponding to Lysozyme release in wound fluid over 144 h at 12-h intervals. Fig. S7(c) depicts the concentration of released Lysozyme in relation to its absorbance maxima. Finally, Fig. S7(d) shows the percentage release of Lysozyme over time, indicating a gradual increase in concentration, with 49 % of Lysozyme released after 144 h. The histopathological analysis clearly demonstrates the role of Lysozyme in the formation of fibro-collagen and granulation tissue in both PVP/CMC-Lys and PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD groups, which are absent in the control experiment. The white circle highlights areas with a high population of granulation tissue, while the green circle indicates fibro-collagen tissue, emphasizing the significant contribution of Lysozyme to the wound healing process. It also shows minimal infiltration of inflammatory cells, as can be clearly visualized in Fig. 6(a). The results for day 14 shows notable alignment of fibroblast and collagen fibrous bundles, without any signs of fibrosis at wound site, that evidently leads to faster wound closure for PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels (Table 1 ). Additionally, the nanocomposite hydrogel inhibits bacterial growth, thereby promoting faster healing rates by enhancing bacterial growth suppression. The inclusion of polymer-modified graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) increases their ability to eliminate bacteria via effective attachment with bacterial cell-membranes. The non-toxic nature of PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposites make it highly compatible with living cells, ensuring it is safe for in vivo trials. These factors position the PVP/CMC-Lys@N-GQD nanocomposite hydrogels as highly promising materials in wound healing applications in higher animal models.

The ability of lysozyme to stimulate faster formation of granulation tissues makes it an attractive option for wound healing applications. In this study, a nanocomposite comprising N-GQD integrated into PVP/CMC, along with lysozyme, was synthesized using low-cost and environmentally friendly techniques. The nanocomposite displayed uniform N-GQD distribution, with no signs of agglomeration, leading to enhanced swelling capacity in wound fluids and an increased water vapor transmission (WVT) rate. The biocompatible material showed significant antibacterial properties against E. coli. However, the material did not exhibit inhibitory effects against Gram-positive strains, which is a limitation of the present investigation. This limitation is attributed to the thick peptidoglycan layer in the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria. However, this challenge can be addressed by further optimizing the material by altering the surface properties of nanomaterials. In vivo wound healing experiments using Drosophila as a model organism demonstrated complete wound closure within just 5 h. Additionally, the assay on rat models revealed complete healing of excision wounds within 14 days which provides encouraging insights towards employment of the nanocomposite hydrogels for wound healing in higher animals.
